title: Whose Drum This Is
summary: In a museum office in Edinburgh, a curator and a visiting representative of the community the drum was taken from disagree about what 'returning' an object means — and discover that the hardest question is not who owns it but what it is for.

body.speaker: Dr Fiona Greer
body.text: I want to begin by saying the museum no longer disputes that the drum should go back. That argument is over. What we are here to work out is how, and what 'back' even means. Because the village it left in 1901 is not the village that exists today.

body.speaker: Tomás Quispe
body.text: With respect, Doctor, that last sentence is the one your institution always reaches for. 'The community has changed.' It is true, and it is also the oldest reason for keeping things. Every community has changed. Yours has too. You do not use it to argue that the museum has no claim to its own collection.

body.speaker: Dr Fiona Greer
body.text: That is fair, and I will not hide behind it. Let me put my real worry plainly. The drum is currently the only one of its kind that survives intact. If it returns and is used — actually played in ceremony, as your council intends — it will degrade. Within two generations it may be gone. Here, it lasts five hundred years. I am not defending ownership. I am asking about the object's future.

body.speaker: Tomás Quispe
body.text: And there we reach the real disagreement, which is not about ownership at all. You are asking how to make the drum last. We are asking what the drum is for. To you a drum that is never played but survives five hundred years is a success. To us it is a drum that has been dead for five hundred years. You would preserve the body and lose the life. We would spend the body to keep the life.

body.speaker: Dr Fiona Greer
body.text: I hear that, and it genuinely unsettles me, because by my professional values you have just described vandalism, and by yours I have just described embalming. Both of us think the other is destroying it. The difference is only in what we think 'it' is.

body.speaker: Tomás Quispe
body.text: Yes. Exactly that. And notice who has had the power, for a hundred and twenty years, to decide which definition counts. The drum has been 'it is an object' because it has been in a place that can only treat it as an object. Give it back and the definition changes — not because we are right and you are wrong, but because the thing finally sits in the context that made it.

body.speaker: Dr Fiona Greer
body.text: Then let me ask something I should perhaps not ask. Is there a version your council would accept in which the drum is recorded, photographed, fully documented, even 3D-scanned before it goes — so that when it is eventually played to dust, something remains?

body.speaker: Tomás Quispe
body.text: We would accept that, and we have offered it before; it was your predecessors who refused, because a scan is worthless to a museum that wants the thing. But understand what you are really asking. You want a copy for the same reason you always wanted the original: so that the object outlives the people. We are letting you keep that, the copy, the part you most value. We are simply taking back the part we most value, which was never the wood.

body.speaker: Tomás Quispe
body.text: The drum is not the wood, Doctor. The drum is the sound a particular person is allowed to make on a particular day. You have been preserving the violin and calling it the music.

grammarNotes.title: Parallel antithesis and the redefining 'is not X; it is Y'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cuộc đối thoại dựng tranh luận bằng phép đối song song B2. Quispe đối xứng hai thế giới quan: 'You are asking how to make the drum last. We are asking what the drum is for', rồi 'You would preserve the body and lose the life. We would spend the body to keep the life'. Greer phản chiếu cấu trúc ấy: 'by my professional values you have just described vandalism, and by yours I have just described embalming'. Câu chốt 'The drum is not the wood … The drum is the sound a particular person is allowed to make on a particular day' dùng cấu trúc tái-định-nghĩa 'is not X; it is Y' và mệnh đề quan hệ rút gọn. Đây là tranh biện đạo đức trừu tượng, nhiều sắc thái, cấp B2.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue argues through B2 parallel antithesis. Quispe mirrors two worldviews: 'You are asking how to make the drum last. We are asking what the drum is for', then 'You would preserve the body and lose the life. We would spend the body to keep the life.' Greer reflects the structure back: 'by my professional values you have just described vandalism, and by yours I have just described embalming.' The closing 'The drum is not the wood … The drum is the sound a particular person is allowed to make on a particular day' uses the redefining 'is not X; it is Y' and a reduced relative clause. This is nuanced, abstract ethical B2 argument.

translationVi:
TS Fiona Greer: Tôi muốn bắt đầu bằng việc nói rằng bảo tàng không còn tranh cãi rằng chiếc trống nên trở về. Cuộc tranh luận đó đã kết thúc. Cái chúng ta ở đây để giải quyết là bằng cách nào, và 'trở về' rốt cuộc nghĩa là gì. Bởi ngôi làng nó rời đi năm 1901 không phải ngôi làng tồn tại hôm nay.

Tomás Quispe: Thưa Tiến sĩ, xin lỗi, câu cuối ấy chính là câu mà tổ chức của cô luôn viện đến. 'Cộng đồng đã thay đổi.' Điều đó đúng, và nó cũng là lý do xưa cũ nhất để giữ lại đồ vật. Mọi cộng đồng đều đã thay đổi. Cộng đồng của cô cũng vậy. Cô đâu dùng nó để lập luận rằng bảo tàng không có quyền với chính bộ sưu tập của mình.

TS Fiona Greer: Đúng đấy, và tôi sẽ không núp sau nó. Để tôi nói thẳng nỗi lo thật của mình. Chiếc trống hiện là cái duy nhất thuộc loại của nó còn nguyên vẹn. Nếu nó trở về và được dùng — thật sự đánh trong nghi lễ, như hội đồng của anh dự định — nó sẽ xuống cấp. Trong vòng hai thế hệ có thể nó sẽ biến mất. Ở đây, nó trụ được năm trăm năm. Tôi không bảo vệ quyền sở hữu. Tôi đang hỏi về tương lai của hiện vật.

Tomás Quispe: Và ở đó ta chạm tới bất đồng thật sự, vốn hoàn toàn không phải về quyền sở hữu. Cô đang hỏi làm sao để chiếc trống trụ lâu. Chúng tôi đang hỏi chiếc trống dùng để làm gì. Với cô, một chiếc trống không bao giờ được đánh nhưng trụ năm trăm năm là một thành công. Với chúng tôi đó là một chiếc trống đã chết năm trăm năm. Cô sẽ bảo tồn cái xác mà đánh mất sự sống. Chúng tôi sẽ tiêu hao cái xác để giữ sự sống.

TS Fiona Greer: Tôi nghe thấy điều đó, và nó thật sự làm tôi bất an, vì theo giá trị nghề nghiệp của tôi anh vừa mô tả hành vi phá hoại, còn theo của anh thì tôi vừa mô tả việc ướp xác. Cả hai chúng ta đều nghĩ người kia đang huỷ hoại nó. Khác biệt chỉ nằm ở chỗ ta nghĩ 'nó' là gì.

Tomás Quispe: Đúng. Chính xác là thế. Và hãy để ý ai đã có quyền lực, suốt một trăm hai mươi năm, để quyết định định nghĩa nào được tính. Chiếc trống là 'một hiện vật' vì nó đã ở một nơi chỉ có thể đối xử với nó như một hiện vật. Trả nó về và định nghĩa thay đổi — không phải vì chúng tôi đúng và cô sai, mà vì vật ấy cuối cùng nằm trong bối cảnh đã tạo ra nó.

TS Fiona Greer: Vậy để tôi hỏi một điều có lẽ tôi không nên hỏi. Có phiên bản nào hội đồng của anh chấp nhận, trong đó chiếc trống được ghi âm, chụp ảnh, ghi chép đầy đủ, thậm chí quét 3D trước khi nó đi — để khi cuối cùng nó được đánh đến tan thành bụi, vẫn còn lại điều gì đó?

Tomás Quispe: Chúng tôi sẽ chấp nhận điều đó, và chúng tôi đã từng đề nghị; chính những người tiền nhiệm của cô đã từ chối, vì một bản quét là vô giá trị với một bảo tàng muốn chính vật ấy. Nhưng hãy hiểu cô thực sự đang hỏi gì. Cô muốn một bản sao vì cùng lý do cô luôn muốn bản gốc: để hiện vật sống lâu hơn con người. Chúng tôi để cô giữ điều đó, bản sao, phần cô quý nhất. Chúng tôi chỉ đơn giản lấy lại phần chúng tôi quý nhất, vốn chưa bao giờ là gỗ.

TS Fiona Greer: Vốn chưa bao giờ là gỗ.

Tomás Quispe: Chiếc trống không phải là gỗ, thưa Tiến sĩ. Chiếc trống là âm thanh mà một người cụ thể được phép tạo ra vào một ngày cụ thể. Cô đã bảo tồn cây vĩ cầm và gọi đó là âm nhạc.

questions.prompt: What does Dr Greer say is no longer disputed?
options: That the drum should go back; the only questions are how and what 'back' means.
questions.explanation: 'the museum no longer disputes that the drum should go back … What we are here to work out is how, and what "back" even means.'

questions.prompt: How does Quispe respond to 'the community has changed'?
options: He agrees it ends the matter.
He says it is true but is the oldest reason for keeping things — every community, including the museum's, has changed.
questions.explanation: 'It is true, and it is also the oldest reason for keeping things. Every community has changed. Yours has too.'

questions.prompt: What is Greer's 'real worry', stated plainly?
options: That the drum is the only intact one of its kind, and if played in ceremony it will degrade and may be gone within two generations.
That the village will sell it.
That the museum will lose funding.
questions.explanation: 'If it returns and is used … it will degrade. Within two generations it may be gone.'

questions.prompt: Where does Quispe locate 'the real disagreement'?
options: Not in ownership at all but in whether the question is how to make the drum last or what the drum is for.
questions.explanation: 'the real disagreement, which is not about ownership at all. You are asking how to make the drum last. We are asking what the drum is for.'

questions.prompt: How does Quispe contrast the two views of an unplayed surviving drum?
options: To Greer it is a success; to his people it is a drum that has been dead for five hundred years.
questions.explanation: 'To you a drum that is never played but survives five hundred years is a success. To us it is a drum that has been dead.'

questions.prompt: How does Greer summarise the clash of values?
options: By her values he described vandalism; by his she described embalming; each thinks the other is destroying it, differing only on what 'it' is.
questions.explanation: 'you have just described vandalism, and by yours I have just described embalming … The difference is only in what we think "it" is.'

questions.prompt: What point about power does Quispe make?
options: For 120 years the holder has had the power to decide which definition counts; the drum is 'an object' because it sat where it could only be treated as one.
questions.explanation: 'who has had the power, for a hundred and twenty years, to decide which definition counts.'

options: Recording, photographing, documenting, even 3D-scanning the drum before it goes, so something remains.
questions.explanation: 'recorded, photographed, fully documented, even 3D-scanned before it goes.'

questions.prompt: What does Quispe say about that scanning offer?
options: His council would accept it and had offered it before; Greer's predecessors refused because a scan is worthless to a museum that wants the thing.
questions.explanation: 'we have offered it before; it was your predecessors who refused, because a scan is worthless to a museum that wants the thing.'

questions.prompt: How does Quispe reinterpret the museum's desire for a copy?
options: As the same wish as always — that the object outlive the people; he lets them keep the copy while taking back the part that was never the wood.
questions.explanation: 'You want a copy for the same reason you always wanted the original: so that the object outlives the people.'

questions.prompt: What does Quispe say the drum actually is?
options: The wood it is made of.
The sound a particular person is allowed to make on a particular day.
questions.explanation: 'The drum is the sound a particular person is allowed to make on a particular day.'

questions.prompt: What is the meaning of 'You have been preserving the violin and calling it the music'?
options: The museum kept the physical container while mistaking it for the living practice it housed.
Drums and violins are the same.
questions.explanation: It is a metaphor: the object is the instrument; the value is the living sound/use.

questions.prompt: The contrast 'preserve the body / spend the body' captures:
options: Two definitions of preservation: keeping the physical object intact versus using it to keep a living practice alive.
questions.explanation: Body = physical object; life = the practice it serves.

questions.prompt: Which claim is the dialogue NOT making?
options: That 'the community has changed' can be a self-serving reason to keep objects.
That the central question is simply who legally owns the drum.
That an object's meaning depends on its context.
That a scan can preserve form without preserving use.
questions.explanation: Quispe says the real disagreement 'is not about ownership at all.'
questions.hint: Find the framing both speakers move past.

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closest to where the two arrive?
options: The museum should keep the drum for safety.
The dispute is less about ownership than about what the drum is — a thing to preserve or a practice to perform — and the museum can keep the copy while the people reclaim the living use.
The drum has no value at all.
questions.explanation: It captures the shift from ownership to definition and the copy/use split.
questions.hint: Combine the fourth and eighth turns.

questions.prompt: How do the two speakers relate?
options: Each holds a coherent value the other partly recognises; Greer fears decay, Quispe values use, and both grant the other's logic.
They reach no understanding at all.
questions.explanation: Greer says it 'genuinely unsettles me'; Quispe concedes the scan offer — mutual recognition.
questions.hint: Note the mutual unsettling and concessions.

cloze.template:
Dr Greer: The museum no longer {{b1}} that the drum should go back. What we are here to work out is how, and what 'back' even means. The village it left in 1901 is not the village that exists today.

Tomás Quispe: That last sentence is the one your institution always {{b2}} for. 'The community has changed.' It is the oldest reason for keeping things. Every community has changed. Yours has too.

Dr Greer: That is fair, and I will not {{b3}} behind it. The drum is the only one of its kind that survives {{b4}}. If it is played in ceremony, it will {{b5}}. I am not defending ownership. I am asking about the object's {{b6}}.

Tomás Quispe: There we reach the real disagreement, which is not about ownership at all. You are asking how to make the drum last. We are asking what the drum is {{b7}}. You would preserve the body and lose the {{b8}}. We would spend the body to keep the life.

Dr Greer: By my professional values you have just described {{b9}}, and by yours I have just described {{b10}}. The difference is only in what we think 'it' is.

Tomás Quispe: Notice who has had the power to decide which {{b11}} counts. Give it back and the definition changes, because the thing finally sits in the context that {{b12}} it.

Dr Greer: Is there a version in which the drum is fully documented, even 3D-{{b13}}, before it goes?

Tomás Quispe: We would accept that. But you want a copy so that the object {{b14}} the people. We are taking back the part we most value, which was never the wood. The drum is the sound a particular person is allowed to {{b15}} on a particular day.

blanks.explanation: I will not hide behind it.
So that the object outlives the people.
Allowed to make on a particular day.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Quispe accuses the museum of 'preserving the violin and calling it the music' — keeping the physical object while losing the living practice it was made for. Think of something your own community or family preserves: a recipe written but never cooked, an instrument displayed but never played, a language spoken only in records. Is the object the thing of value, or is the value in the use that wears it out? If you could only keep one — the perfectly preserved object or the living practice that destroys it — which would you choose, and what does that choice say about what you think the thing actually is?
